The three presently recognized subspecies (roxellana, hubeiensis, qinlingensis) differ from one another mainly in tail length, as well as in certain features of skeletal structure and width of dental arch (Groves 2001). No genetic separation into distinct clades and therefore the sub-specific designation.
No subspecies or geographic variants have been described.

This species belongs to the silenus species group, and was formerly known as Macaca maurus. It hybridizes with M. tonkeana in areas where their ranges overlap at the base of the Toraja Highlands (Tempe depression).
Presbytis canicrus was previously included as a subspecies of P. hosei, but it is now recognised as a separate species (Roos et al. 2014). This is a monotypic species.

This taxon was classified as a subspecies of the Phayre’s Langur (Trachypithecus phayrei). Genetic data suggest that this species is a product of ancient hybridisation between the ancestors of the francoisi- and the obscurus-group (Liedigk et al. 2009). More recent genetic studies indicate that the genetic material acquired from the obscurus-group is specifically from the Tenasserim Langur (T. barbei) and not from T. phayrei (Roos, unpubl.).There is some geographic variation. Individuals from the northeastern part of the distribution tend to be darker, particularly on the limbs and tail.Although it is now accepted as a valid species (T. crepuscula), the taxon is still listed as a subspecies on The IUCN Red list because the revised taxonomic concept of T. phayrei has not yet been assessed by the IUCN SSC Primate Specialist Group.

Three elephant taxa remain from the sixteen elephant-like species that are known from across the planet in the Pleistocene: Asian Elephant (Elephas maximus), African Savanna Elephant (Loxodonta africana), and African Forest Elephant (Loxodonta cyclotis) (Faurby and Svenning 2015, Malhi et al. 2016). The Asian and African ancestral lineages diverged approximately seven million years ago, and the African Savanna and African Forest ancestral lineages began diverging approximately one million years later (Rohland et al. 2010, Brandt et al. 2014, Roca et al. 2015, Meyer et al. 2017, Palkopoulou et al. 2018). The Third Edition of ‘Mammal Species of the World’ (Wilson and Reeder 2005) was the first to formally designate the African elephant as these two separate species. Recent genetic findings also support this designation (Roca et al. 2007, Ishida et al. 2011, Mondol et al. 2015, Palkopoulou et al. 2018, Kim and Wasser 2019). Hybridization between the two species appears restricted and evident at only 14 of the more than 100 localities recently examined across the vast forest-savanna ecotone. In nine of these 14 localities, hybrid individuals occurred alongside non-hybrid individuals of either one species or the other and not both (i.e., three localities had hybrids and African Forest Elephants only and assigned as this species; six localities had hybrids and African Savanna Elephants only and assigned as this species). For the IUCN Red List assessments, a distribution map published in Mondol et al. (2015) and recent data by Kim and Wasser (2019) are used to assign localities as range of either L. africana or L. cyclotis.

Presbytis percura was described by M.W. Lyon, Jr. in 1908 based on specimens collected from near Siak Kecil River, Makapan, Kompei, Pulau Rupat, and Salat Rupat (Lyon 1908). It was later regarded as a subspecies of P. femoralis, along with P. f. femoralis and P. f. robinsoni (Groves 2001, Meyer et al. 2011, Groves and Roos 2013, Roos et al. 2014). However, genetic data suggested that at least P. f. femoralis and P. f. robinsoni were different species (Ang 2010, Nijman 2019) which was also in agreement with their morphological characters.Resolving all subspecies-level boundaries within banded langurs required data for P. f. percura, which was the least studied among the three taxa and only found in the Riau Province in Sumatra (Rizaldi et al. 2019). Complete mitochondrial genomes were later obtained for P. f. percura in the wild, and based on multiple species delimitation algorithms (PTP, ABGD, Objective Clustering) applied to a dataset covering 40 species and 43 subspecies of Asian colobines, all three subspecies of banded langurs were resurrected to species (Ang et al. 2020).Presbytis percura is mainly blackish above, with a grey head and a white underside (Lyon 1908, Aimi and Bakar 1992). White underside is narrow but extends horizontally across chest, down inner sides of limbs in thin stripes to wrists and ankles, and to cheeks and chin. The tail is black and not bicolour, but part of the ventral surface of the basal third of the tail is indistinctly white. Its throat and face are grey, and there are narrow, pale eye-rings. One (sometimes two) whorls are set well back on its crown. Hairs on the chest are directed backward.The foetuses are white with a black stripe from the head to the tail, intersected by another black stripe along the outer surfaces of both forearms, forming a distinctive cruciform (cross-like) black pattern on the dorsum.

Macaca leucogenys was first described by Li et al. (2015) based on photos captured by camera traps, and its full species status was confirmed based on DNA extracted from skin specimens (Fan et al. 2017).Macaca leucogenys is distinguished from all potential sympatric macaque species (M. mulatta, M. thibetana, M. assamensis, and M. munzala) based on a suite of pelage characteristics including relatively uniform dorsal hair pattern, hairy ventral pelage, relatively hairless short tail, prominent pale to white side- and chin-whiskers creating a white cheek and round facial appearance, dark facial skin on the muzzle, long and thick hairs on its neck, and a round rather than arrow-shaped male genitalia.

This taxon was considered a subspecies of Nycticebus coucang but elevated by Groves and Maryanto (2008). This is further supported by morphological studies by Nekaris and Jaffe (2007) and Nekaris and Munds (2010) and genetic studies by Roos (2003) and Wirdateti et al. (2006). Two distinct forms regularly occur in bird markets and further research is needed (Nekaris and Jaffe 2007). Care should be taken in taxonomic studies however due to translocation of individuals from markets into forests and gardens throughout the island (Schulze and Groves 2004). The distinct black and white long-haired appearance of subadults and juveniles also might be mistaken as separate species, for example, in museum specimens or from photos only (KA. Nekaris et al. pers. comm. 2012).

Four species of Lion Tamarins are recognized. Coimbra-Filho (1990) suggested that Leontopithecus caissara was a subspecies, or colour variant of L. chrysopygus. Burity et al. (1999) and Perez-Sweeney et al. (2008) concluded that this was not so, and that L. caissara is a valid species. Perez-Sweeney et al. (2008) concluded that there are three unambiguous clades revealed by phylogenetic analysis of mtDNA control region sequences: Leontopithecus chysomelas, L. caissara and L. chrysopygus/L. rosalia. According to these authors, L. chysomelas occupies a basal phylogenetic position. Here we follow the taxonomy proposed by Rylands (2012).
Myotis lucifugus does not include occultus. This species hybridizes with M. yumanensis in some areas; see Parkinson (1979). Apparently it is closely related to M. thysanodes.

Rice’s Whale (Balaenoptera ricei) is a distinct evolutionary lineage closely related to Eden’s Whale (Balaenoptera edeni edeni), Bryde’s Whale (B. e. brydei) and Sei Whale (Balaenoptera borealis) (Rosel et al. 2021). Previously considered an undescribed subspecies of Balaenoptera edeni and referred to as the Gulf of Mexico Whale or the Gulf of Mexico Bryde’s Whale, a recent morphological study of Rice’s Whale has revealed diagnostic differences in the shape and size of bones in the skull in comparison to the two subspecies of B. edeni. This morphological evidence reinforced earlier findings of significant genetic divergence and fixed DNA differences found in several mitochondrial DNA genes; together, these data supported the description of the new species B. ricei. With elevation of Rice’s Whale to species status, there is still uncertainty as to whether Eden’s Whale and Bryde’s Whale, each of which is clearly distinct, represent subspecies of B. edeni or full species.

Rhinolophus montanus was originally described as a subspecies of philippinensis (Goodwin 1979), but was recently elevated to species status (Csorba 2002). This taxonomy is generally accepted and followed (Csorba et al. 2003, Simmons 2005).

This taxon has in the past sometimes been included as a subspecies of Pipistrellus tenuis (Koopman 1973, 1993), but its status has been confirmed by genetic and morphological assessment, which revealed it was a full species endemic to Christmas Island (Helgen et al. 2009).

This species was previously considered a subspecies of Chaerephon jobensis (Felten 1964, Hill 1983) but has been recognised as a distinct species since its treatment by Flannery (1995). The taxonomy is considered stable (Simmons 2005) despite the absence of full taxonomic enquiry.

Woodman (1993) notes that many mammalian generic names ending in -otis use the wrong gender for specific names. If this advice is to be followed, the species name should be M. pruinosa. The Chiroptera SG advise keeping the names as they are for now while a decision is still to be made on these.
This species is closely related to Myotis pruinosus that occurs in mainland Japan (Kawai et al. 2003, Abe et al. 2005).

Cercopithecus dryas is one of Africa’s least known and most enigmatic monkeys. Its nomenclature has been disputed, and its phylogenetic affinities are uncertain. The type specimen, the skull and skin of a captive juvenile male from the early 1930s, is from an unknown source population in central Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), most likely in the Lomela region in what is currently Sankuru Province (Schwarz 1932). The species was not reported again until 1977, when Kano and Kuroda acquired two hunter-killed animals in the Wamba research area 300 km north of Lomela town (Kuroda et al. 1985). One of these animals became the type specimen for a new species: Cercopithecus salongo Thys van den Audenaerde 1977. Subsequent re-examination of the C. dryas type and Wamba specimen indicated that these represented the same species with age-related differences in colouration, thus sinking salongo into dryas (Colyn et al. 1991). Sarmiento (2002) disputed this assessment. However, the current treatment follows Groves (2001, 2005), Grubb et al. (2003), Butynski (2013), and Healy (2013) in accepting the synonymy of the two forms. Following the collections by Kano and Kuroda, C. dryas was confirmed in the Kokolopori area to the east of Wamba (Lokasola 2008). Lokasola (2008) suggests that Kano and Kuroda’s 1977 specimen likely came from this same population.In 2014, the species reappeared again, 400 km southeast of Kokolopori/Wamba near Bafundo, in Maniema Province, less than 15 km west of the Congo (Lualaba) River. Field staff from the Lukuru Foundation’s TL2 Project collected photos of a fresh hunter-killed adult female and later collected a hunter-killed adult male from this same area (Hart 2014). Subsequently, the species was seen at two sites ~31 km west of Bafundo near the Lomami River, and two sites ~75 km west of the city of Kindu (Alempijevic et al. 2018). Both sexes of the Lomami-Lualaba interfluve population are characterized by striking rufous cheeks and upper ventrum, a feature not mentioned in the published descriptions of C. dryas sensu lato, as all publications describe specimens with white- or cream-coloured facial hairs and ventrum (Schwarz 1932, Thys van den Audenaerde 1977, Kuroda et al. 1985). Nevertheless, a photo of a monkey taken in 2011 at Iyondji in the Kokolopori/Wamba region by R. Mittermeier clearly shows these animals have rufous cheeks. Subsequently, S. Kuroda (comment posted online in 2016 in Hart 2014) confirmed that his 1977 Wamba specimens looked like the Bafundo animals. Tail colouration, in particular the shape and location of the triangular dark grey ventral patch at the base of the tail, in both sexes, has also been a source of confusion (e.g. C. dryas illustration, Gautier-Hion et al. 1999). The prominent blue perineum and scrotum in adult males (Hart 2014) has not been mentioned in previous published accounts of the species, but this feature likely fades in death, as in C. lomamiensis, and may not be evident in all hunter-killed animals and dry museum skins (Hart et al. 2012). Additional field surveys and population genetics studies are needed to assess the level of variation present among known populations, as this monkey occurs in very small, widely-separated populations over a very large area, and with no evident contact between them.Cercopithecus dryas is currently assigned to its own species group, the Cercopithecus dryas-group (Groves 2001, Butynski 2002), where it is the smallest species within the genus Cercopithecus (Butynski 2013). However, recent phylogenetic analyses based on the mitochondrial genome of the type specimen (finger tip) show C. dryas clustering within the genus Chlorocebus (Guschanski et al. 2013). Groves suggested possible affinities between C. dryas and Chlorocebus after initial assessment of the skin and skull of the type specimen (1972 pers. comm. cited in Thys van den Audenaerde 1977), however he later concluded that C. dryas is a distinct species within the genus Cercopithecus based on the cranial morphology of the type specimen and a photograph of an adult skull in Kuroda et al. (1985). A study of the morphology by Gilbert et al. (2021) confirmed the results of Guschanski et al. (2013) in treating the dryas monkey as a member of the genus Chlorocebus, not Cercopithecus (as per Groves 2001). Note also that the phylogenomic study of Guschanski et al. (2013) placed Allochrocebus solatus (Harrison, 1988) of Gabon as basal to the Chlorocebus clade.

Modern taxonomic arrangements of the colobus monkeys either distinguish the red colobus monkeys and the Olive Colobus as separate genera, Piliocolobus and Procolobus, respectively (e.g., Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Roos et al. 2011, Kingdon 2015), or consider them to belong to one genus Procolobus, with two subgenera (Procolobus for the Olive Colobus and Piliocolobus for the red colobus) (Oates et al. 1994, Grubb et al. 2003, 2013; Oates 2011). Ting (2008) placed all the red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) badius. The 2008 IUCN Red List assessments followed the taxonomy of Grubb et al. (2003).The arrangement of two genera in Groves (2001, 2005, 2007) is followed here: a monotypic Procolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (Olive Colobus) and polytypic Piliocolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (red colobus).This taxon was formerly a subspecies of a wider concept of P. pennantii that included as subspecies parmentierorum, bouvieri, tholloni, powelli Matschie, nigrimanus Trouessart, lulindicus, langi, foai and semlikiensis (Gautier-Hion et al. 1999). Kingdon (1997), Groves (2001, 2005) and Butynski et al. (2013) considered bouvieri and epieni to be subspecies of pennantii; Grubb et al. (2003) likewise, and also included preussi.

With the revisions and reviews of sportive lemurs located in northwestern Madagascar between 2006 and 2007, the distribution of Lepilemur dorsalis and other sportive lemurs in this region remained uncertain. Zinner et al. (2007) showed that the three teams that described new Lepilemur species in 2006 and 2007, Andriaholinirina et al. (2006), Louis et al. (2006) and Craul et al. (2007) had each submitted three alternative concepts of the holotype locality for L. dorsalis and that the validity of L. tymerlachsoni and L. mittermeieri depended on which concept turned out to be correct. The type locality of Lepilemur dorsalis was thought to be either Mourountsang (=Anorontsangana, 13°55’S, 47°55’E) or Passandava (=Ampasindava, about 13°40’S, 48°15’E). If the former was correct, then L. tymerlachsonorum is a synonym of L. dorsalis and L. mittermeieri is valid; and if the latter, then L. mittermeieri is the synonym and L. tymerlachsoni is valid. The species found at Nosy Bé is also found on Anorontsangana (Zinner et al. 2007). Further, the holotype L. grandidieri (Forsyth Major 1894) was attributed to a specimen from northern Madagascar, which may prove to be a senior synonym of one of the species described in 2006/2007. Therefore, it was recommended that as a priority research and conservation study, genetic material from the holotypes for L. dorsalis and L. grandidieri be acquired and comparable sequence data generated to determine the taxonomy and distribution of L. dorsalis and the other sportive lemurs of north-west Madagascar.Recent phylogenetic studies by Roos et al. (2021) confirmed that L. mittermeieri and L. grandidieri Forsyth Major, 1894, are both junior synonyms of L. dorsalis Gray, 1871. The authors sequenced the complete mitochondrial genomes of the L. dorsalis and L. grandidieri (currently recognized as a synonym of L. dorsalis) type specimens and compared the sequences with those of samples with known provenance from the different Inter-River Systems (IRSs). The two type specimens of L. dorsalis and L. grandidieri are identical and cluster closely with samples collected in IRS V (between the rivers Andranomalaza and Sambirano), indicating that the type locality can be fixed to IRS V. Consequently, L. dorsalis occurs in IRS V, and L. grandidieri and L. mittermeieri are junior synonyms of L. dorsalis.

Until recently, Trachypithecus margarita was included in T. germaini. Genetic data (mtDNA studies) and phenotypical characters supports separate species status, though additional study will be needed for confirmation, since the study was based on a few samples from Cambodia and Viet Nam (including samples from animals of unknown origin) and no samples from Lao PDR (Roos et al., 2008). It is considered to be part of the cristatus group (T. cristatus, T. auratus, T. mauritus, T. germaini and T. selangorensis) (Hoang Minh Duc et al. 2012, Nadler and Brockman 2014, Nadler et al. 2005, Roos et al. 2008).
Bradypus pygmaeus has only recently been described by Anderson and Handley (2001) as a separate species.
Unidentified Pipistrellus bats found in the Azores have recently been proposed to belong to this species, but this awaits confirmation.
Identified as a new species by genetic analyses, this species combines morphological features of both P. austriacus and P. auritus.

Here we refer to P. teneriffae as the population of the Canary Islands. Northwest African populations previously assigned to P. teneriffae are currently included in P. gaisleri, and Balkan populations are assigned to P. austriacus. The taxonomic status of the species was confirmed by Spitzenberger et al. (2006). There are no significant genetic differences between populations on the islands of the Canary Archipelago where this species is found (Juste et al. 2004).

Genetic (Rosel et al. 1995) and morphological (Brownell et al. 1987) data suggest that Vaquitas are most closely related to porpoises in South America. Genetic data suggest divergence from two sister taxa (Burmeister’s Porpoise, Phocoena spinipinnis and Spectacled Porpoise Australophocaena dioptrica) in the Pleistocene (i.e., at least 2.5 million years ago).

The intraspecific taxonomy of the Tiger (Panthera tigris) has been recently debated due to inconsistent results from various methodologies (Luo et al. 2004, Wilting et al. 2015, Kitchener et al. 2017, Liu et al. 2018). Luo et al. (2004) identify six subspecies on the analysis of mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) while Wilting et al. (2015) classified Tigers into two subspecies based on data sets of several traits [morphological (craniodental and pelage), ecological, molecular]. These inconsistencies were partly attributed to the lack of genetic samples across the Tiger range. However, Liu et al. (2018) used whole-genome sequencing analyses from 32 voucher specimens that resolve six statistically robust monophyletic clades. The higher resolution of data obtained from nuclear genomes highlights little admixture and gene flow between the six subspecies. Nevertheless, there is incomplete congruence between mitochondrial and nuclear DNA results for P. t. amoyensis, suggesting the inclusion of more samples in future analysis to confirm its relationship with P. t. jacksoni.Liu et al. (2018) concluded that six extant subspecies exist based on genome-wide analysis:Amur Tiger (P. t. altaica): Russian Far East and northeastern China.Northern Indochinese Tiger (P. t. corbetti): Indochina north of the Malayan Peninsula.Malayan Tiger (P. t. jacksoni): Peninsular Malaysia.Sumatran Tiger (P. t. sumatrae): Sumatra.Bengal Tiger (P. t. tigris): Indian sub-continent.South China Tiger (P. t. amoyensis), although this subspecies has not been directly observed in the wild since the 1970s and is possibly extinct.Three subspecies previously recognised based on morphology are extinct:Bali Tiger (P. t. balica Schwarz, 1912): Bali. Javan Tiger (P. t. sondaica (Temminck, 1844)): Java.Caspian Tiger (P. t. virgata (Illiger, 1815)): dry river valleys of the Takla Makan, western slopes of the Tianshan mountains, Amudarya and Syrdarya river valleys, shores of the Caspian Sea, Elburz mountains, eastern Turkey, Tigris and Euphrates River valleys.However, given the varied interpretations of existing data, the taxonomy of this species is currently under review by the IUCN SSC Cat Specialist Group.
Microcebus berthae is the name given to the diminutive Rufous-colored Mouse Lemur from Kirindy/CFPF, originally called M. myoxinus by Schmid and Kappeler (1994).

Presbytis femoralis formerly was regarded as a subspecies of P. melalophos (Thorington and Groves 1970, Medway 1983, Napier 1985, Oates et al. 1994), but later was recognized as a species with three subspecies: P. f. femoralis, P. f. percura and P. f. robinsoni (Groves 2001, Meyer et al. 2011, Groves and Roos 2013, Roos et al. 2014).Genetic data suggested that at least P. f. femoralis and P. f. robinsoni were different species (Ang 2010, Nijman 2019), which also agreed with their morphological characters. However, resolving all subspecies-level boundaries within banded langurs required data for P. f. percura, which was the least studied and only found in the Riau Province in Sumatra (Rizaldi et al. 2019). Complete mitochondrial genomes were later obtained for P. f. percura in the wild, and based on multiple species delimitation algorithms (PTP, ABGD, Objective Clustering) applied to a dataset covering 40 species and 43 subspecies of Asian colobines, all three subspecies of banded langurs were resurrected to species (Ang et al. 2020).Presbytis femoralis was described by Martin (1838) based on specimens collected by Raffles (1821) from Singapore. However, Martin (1838) gave the distribution as "Sumatra etc.", not mentioning Singapore explicitly, resulting in some confusion over the actual type locality (Miller 1934, Low and Lim 2015). Miller (1934) determined Singapore as the actual type locality, and the secretariat of the International Code of Zoological Nomenclature confirmed the authorship of P. femoralis as Martin, the year of publication as 1838, and the type locality as Singapore (Low and Lim 2015).Presbytis femoralis is uniform black on the dorsum with a white band traversing down the chest and abdomen, the inside of the humeri from the axilla, and the inside of the thighs (Raffles 1821, Martin 1838, Groves 2001). In contrast to the adult pelage, infants are born with a white coat with a broad black band from the head, along the spine, to the tail, and intersected by a black band passing along the shoulders to the outer surfaces of both forearms, forming a distinctive cruciform (cross-like) black pattern on the dorsum. A faint black line runs from the dark spinal stripe along the hind legs to the feet. The crown and facial hair are completely white from the frontal view. The forehead is slightly dark compared to the pale colour of the face (Pocock 1928, Ang et al. 2010).

The use of Lutra for four New World otter species (L. felina, L. canadensis, L. longicaudis, L. provocax) has been widespread in the past. Van Zyll de Jong (1972, 1987) separated this group into the genus Lontra based on morphological criteria, which was confirmed by Wozencraft (1993). Koepfli and Wayne (1998) and Koepfli et al. (2008) re-confirmed this classification based on molecular data. Thus, nowadays the generic name Lontra should be accepted as valid for all New World otters (except Pteronura).
Rhinopithecus brelichi was formerly considered a subspecies of Rhinopithecus roxellana, but is now accepted as specifically distinct (Groves 2001).

A second species of Hypogeomys is known from the subfossil record. Hypogeomys australis was still extant 4,400 years ago, and once occurred from southeastern Madagascar north to at least the Antsirabe region (Goodman and Rakotondravony 1996).

authority: Simmons, Flanders, Fils, Parker, Suter, Bamba, Douno, Keita, Morales, & Frick, 2021
taxonomicNotes: Species first described in 2021 after two individuals were caught in the Nimba Mountains in January 2018 as part of a biodiversity assessment. While Myotis nimbaensis shares similar features with three other large Myotis (subgenus Chrysopteron) species with orange dorsal fur occur in Africa (M. tricolor, M. welwitschii, and M. morrisi), it is easily distinguishable due to its body size and various color patterns (Simmons et al. 2021).
Recent taxonomic revision led to elevation from subspecies (Perameles bougainville myosorus) to species status (Travouillon and Phillips 2018).
Recent taxonomic revision led to elevation from subspecies (Perameles bougainville notina) to species status (Travouillon and Phillips 2018).
Formerly included within P. bougainville, but recent taxonomic review recognised its distinct specific status (Travouillon and Phillips 2018), based on a set of morphological criteria.

authority: Travouillon, Simoes, Miguez, Brace, Brewer, Stemmer, Price, Cramb & Louys, 2019
taxonomicNotes: Formerly included within C. ecaudatus, the specific status was recognised by Travouillon et al. (2019).

The taxonomic treatment of Petauroides volans is contested and not fully resolved. A single species has long been accepted. However, Jackson (2015) and Jackson and Groves (2015) recognised three species: Petauroides volans, P. minor and P. armillatus, based on limited published evidence. A subsequent genetic study provided some support to this treatment (McGregor et al. 2020), and the three species are currently listed in the American Society of Mammalogists Mammal Diversity Database (version 1.13). In its most recent conservation assessment, the Australian government recognised two species: P. volans (including P. armillatus) and P. minor (Threatened Species Scientific Committee 2022a,b). However, that split is not recognised in a recent taxonomic review of Australasian marsupials (Baker et al. 2023), by the Australasian Mammal Taxonomy Consortium (as at August 2024) and, following this, by Jackson (2023). The previous Red List account was for the single species, P. volans; until the taxonomy is fully resolved, and the IUCN SSC Australasian Marsupial and Monotreme Specialist Group continues to follow this treatment.

Spalax istricus was previously listed as a subspecies of S. graecus but has since been recognized as a separate species due to recent molecular genetic and detailed morphological studies (Németh et al. 2013).The three known subpopulations, separated by the Carpathian Mountains, can be regarded as separate species, and assigned to already described taxa, this includes the Oltenia Blind Mole-rat.

Until recently, the silvered langurs of Indochina were considered conspecific with the Sundaic Silvered Langur (Trachypithecus cristatus), which was thought to be widely distributed across parts on Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, Viet Nam, Cambodia, Lao PDR, and Myanmar. During the past few years, genetic analyses have supported the assignment of all non-Sundaic populations to the species T. germaini, and subsequently called for a further division of the Indochinese taxon into two species (Groves 2001, Nadler et al. 2005, Roos et al. 2008). The Indochinese populations occurring west of the Mekong River in Viet Nam, Cambodia, Lao PDR, Thailand, and Myanmar have been  attributed to T. germaini, or the Indochinese Silvered Langur, while populations occurring east of the Mekong River in Viet Nam, Cambodia, and Lao PDR have been attributed to T. margarita, or the Annamese Silvered Langur (Hoang Minh Duc et al. 2012a, Nadler and Brockman 2014, Nadler et al. 2005, Roos et al. 2008, Roos et al. 2014). Recent data based on a study of populations separated by the Mekong River provide support for the species split but indicate that the Mekong, while limiting gene flow, is not an impermeable barrier (Moody 2018). Furthermore, this analysis revealed that a population in north-eastern Cambodia was distinguishable compared to other study populations, both west and east of the Mekong. This may be indicative of a species barrier farther east of the Mekong that extends into Viet Nam but additional sampling of populations in both Lao and Viet Nam are critical to better understanding the limits between these species (Moody 2018). Thus, taxonomic analyses should be expanded outside of Cambodia with the aim of clarifying the boundary between these taxa and the limits of their distribution.

Hill (1953) listed six subspecies of Loris tardigradus. Groves (1989) recognized a single species, L. tardigradus, with three “sharply distinct” subspecies in Sri Lanka and two in India (see Schulze and Meier 1995). In 1998, however, Groves argued for two species: the very small and narrow snouted monotypic red form (L. tardigradus) from the wet zone of Sri Lanka, and the larger grey or ashy form (L. lydekkerianus) in India (two subspecies) and Sri Lanka (two subspecies). This taxonomy was followed in Groves (2001, 2005) and Brandon-Jones et al. (2004).A phylogenetic analysis by Pozzi et al. (2015) and a morphological study by Gamage et al. (2017) also confirmed the validity of the two species. In 2004, Nekaris and Jaywardene showed that nycticeboides, also from the wet zone of Sri Lanka, is a subspecies of tardigradus, contra Groves (2001, 2005) and Brandon-Jones et al. (2004), who placed it as a subspecies of lydekkerianus. Gamage et al. (2017) argued that the slender loris from Mirigama, Gampaha District, Western Province of Sri Lanka was unique in its facial features, and cranial and post-cranial morphology, and distinct as such from L. t. tardigradus south of its range. They named it L. t. parvus. They also found evidence for a distinct, high-altitude (above 800 m) taxon in the southwestern wet zone in the Deniyaya-Rakwana mountains, which they declined to name pending more information. As such in 2017, L. tardigradus was believed to comprise three subspecies: L. t. tardigradus, L. t. nycticeboides, and L. t. parvus all from Sri Lanka. This is the taxonomy that is followed by the Primate Specialist Group.Gamage et al. (2019) subsequently carried out a phylogenetic analysis, which they believed largely confirmed this arrangement for the Sri Lankan lorises, but this was shown to be insufficient by Nijman et al. (2020). As such, more robust genetic studies are needed.

Three regional subspecies originally described by Wilson et al. (1991) have been confirmed by Cronin et al. (1996): Enhydra lutris lutris (Linnaeus, 1758) from Japan in the southwest Pacific throughout the Asian range of the Kuril Islands to the Kamchatka Peninsula and the Commander Islands, E. l. kenyoni from the western Aleutian Islands to Prince William Sound, Alaska, USA, along the Pacific coast of Canada and into Oregon state in the continental USA, and E. l. nereis (Merriam, 1904) from central to southern California, USA.

The taxonomy of all the canids, including Lycaon pictus, is currently under review by the Canid Specialist Group. Standard taxonomic texts recognise five subspecies: L. pictus pictus in southern Africa, L. pictus lupinus in eastern Africa, L. pictus somalicus in north-eastern Africa Africa, L. pictus sharicus in central Africa, and L. pictus manguensis in West Africa (Wilson and Reeder 2005). Morphological and genetic data from southern and eastern Africa initially tended to support the pictus/lupinus division (Girman et al. 1993); however, later analyses suggested greater mixing between the two regions (Girman et al. 2001). Ongoing genomic analyses over a broader geographical area (Ramage et al. in prep) will inform the Canid Specialist Group assessment.

It has been argued, on the basis of mandibular measurements, that Crocidura canariensis should be treated as a subspecies of C. sicula (Sarà 1996). However, other morphological, ecological, palaeontological and molecular evidence supports specific status (Michaux et al. 1991, Hutterer et al. 1992). Genetic distances suggest that C. canariensis and C. sicula diverged approximately five million years ago (Vogel et al. 2003), and both share karyotype with C. tarfayaensis (Vogel et al. 2006).
Formerly considered as a subspecies of Sicista subtilis, but now recognized as a separate species based on caryotype and DNA data (Kovalskaya et al. 2011, Lebedev et al. 2020).

For a long time, the westernmost representatives of the genus Spalax were identified as a single species, Spalax graecus. According to the recent studies (Németh et al. 2013, Csorba et al. 2015) the three known subpopulations, separated by the Carpathian Mountains, represent separate species which can be assigned to described taxa. One of them is Spalax antiquus Méhely’s Blind Mole-rat. The separation of Méhely’s Blind Mole-rat as a distinct species is highly supported by the combined evidences of morphology, multilocus phylogeny, biogeographical evidences and species congruence with past tectonic and climatic events (Németh et al. 2013).

According to present taxonomic knowledge, four subspecies are distinguished by Helgen et al. 2009: S. c. citellus; S. c. gradojevici with synonyms karamani and macedonicus; S. c. istricus with synonyms laskarevi and S. c. martinoi which has balcanicus and thracius as synonyms. In a detailed revision of palearctic rodents (Kryštufek and Vohralík 2012) distinguish only three subspecies: s. c. citellus with synonyms istricus, laskarevi and probably also martinoi and balcanicus; s. c. gradojevici with synonyms thracius and macedonicus; and s. c. karamani. The validity of some subspecies should be considered as uncertain until detailed genetic studies are performed.

There are two chromosome races of this species: Spermophilus suslicus odessanus (36 chromosomes) and Spermophilus suslicus sensu stricto (34 chromosomes). These are separated by the Dnieper River. Sometimes considered as separate species, genetic and acoustic data support the integrity of both races into one species (Matrosova et al. 2016).

The species was described as Sminthus loriger Nathusius in Nordmann, 1840, but for a long time was considered as subspecies of Sicista subtilis sensu lato (Sicista subtilis nordmanni). Now this taxon elevated to species level following Zagorodniuk and Selyunina (2009) and Cserkész et al. (2016).Sicista nordmanni (Keyserling & Blasius, 1840) is synonym as it was pointed at by Chaworth-Musters (1934). Nomenclature was revised by Zagorodniuk (2009), and current name “Sminthus loriger Nathusius in Nordmann, 1840” listed as synonym of “Sicista nordmanni Keyserling et Blasius, 1840. Sicista loriger is geographically separated from Sicista subtilis s. str. (Zagorodniuk 2011).

Considering molecular, morphological and biogeographic evidence, Byrne et al. (2016) proposed new genus-level systematics for the former arrangement of Callicebus species from Hershkovitz (1990): Cheracebus Byrne et al. 2016 genus for the species in the Orinoco, Negro and upper Amazon basins; Callicebus Thomas, 1903 genus for the species in the Atlantic Forest; and Plecturocebus Byrne et al. 2016 genus for the species in the Amazon basin and Chaco.Plecturocebus vieirai was described in 2012 from three localities in Pará State, Brazil (type locality: Renato River, a tributary of the right bank of the Teles Pires river, near the city of Cláudia, Mato Grosso; 11° 33' 00.15" S, 55° 10' 59.98" W) (Gualda-Barros et al. 2012), in an area formerly attributed to P. moloch sensu Hershkovitz (1990). The Vieira’s titi monkey is currently known to occur west of the Xingu River, east of the Iriri and Teles Pires rivers, southward to the headwaters of the Xingu and Teles Pires rivers, until the border between Amazonia–Cerrado biomes (Costa-Araújo et al. in press), and it is sister taxon to P. moloch (Boubli et al. 2019).

authority: Costa-Araújo, Silva-Jr., Boubli, Rossi, Hrbek & Farias, 2021
taxonomicNotes: Mico schneideri was described in 2021 for pseudo-cryptic marmosets endemic to the northern Juruena–Teles Pires interfluve, Mato Grosso State, Brazil. Marmosets from this region were stored in the Museu Paraense Emílio Goledi and known to scientists since 1995. They were, however, confounded with M. emiliae due to inadequacy of previous taxonomic assessments––entirely based on pelage colour of a few specimens––until new morphological, molecular and distribution data had shown that they are in fact morphologically diagnosable, form a fully supported clade and are allopatric, without evidence of distribution, morphological or genomic overlap with M. emiliae or another known species of Mico (Costa-Araújo et al. 2021).

Three elephant taxa remain from the sixteen elephant-like species that are known from across the planet in the Pleistocene: Asian Elephant (Elephas maximus), African Savanna Elephant (Loxodonta africana), and African Forest Elephant (Loxodonta cyclotis) (Faurby and Svenning 2015, Malhi et al. 2016). The Asian and African ancestral lineages diverged approximately seven million years ago, and the African Savanna and African Forest ancestral lineages began diverging approximately one million years later (Rohland et al. 2010, Brandt et al. 2014, Roca et al. 2015, Meyer et al. 2017, Palkopoulou et al. 2018). The Third Edition of ‘Mammal Species of the World’ (Wilson and Reeder 2005) was the first to formally designate the African elephant as these two separate species. Recent genetic findings also support this designation (Roca et al. 2007, Ishida et al. 2011, Mondol et al. 2015, Palkopoulou et al. 2018, Kim and Wasser 2019). Hybridization between the two species appears restricted and evident at only 14 of the more than 100 localities recently examined across the vast forest-savanna ecotone. In nine of these 14 localities, hybrid individuals occurred alongside non-hybrid individuals of either one species or the other and not both (i.e., three localities had hybrids and African Forest Elephants only and assigned as this species; six localities had hybrids and African Savanna Elephants only and assigned as this species). For the IUCN Red List assessments, a distribution map published in Mondol et al. (2015) and recent data by Kim and Wasser (2019) are used to assign localities as range of either L. africana or L. cyclotis.

This taxon was formerly treated as a subspecies of the Macaca nemestrina group, which comprised M. n. nemestrina, M. n. leonina, and M. n. pagensis (Fooden 1975, Rosenblum et al. 1997).The boundary zone between M. leonina and M. nemestrina is not well defined and lies at the Surat Thani-Krabi depression (8-9°30'N) where ranges overlap and hybridization is likely to occur (Malaivijitnond et al. 2012). Formerly, there were reports of hybridization with M. leonina in a small area in southern mainland Thailand and on the islands of Phuket and Yao Yai (Fooden 1975, in Groves 2001), however more recent reports do not provide conclusive evidence of presence of a M. nemestrina population on Phuket (iNaturalist 2022, S. Malaivijitnond, pers. comm.). Hybridization of M. nemestrina with M. fascicularis also occurs in Sepilok, Sabah (Gilhooly et al. 2021) and is occasionally observed in other parts of Malaysia (N. Ruppert, pers. obs.).Molecular clock analysis suggests that M. nemestrina arrived in the Malay Peninsula about 0.32 million years ago; and the Bornean populations are suggested to be the first extant lineages to have separated from the other examined lineages of M. nemestrina, M. leonina, M. pagensis, and M. siberu in Southeast Asia (Abdul-Latiff and Md-Zain 2021).The population from Perak (west Peninsular Malaysia) is reported to differ genetically from other extant populations based on phylogenetic and population genetic analyses, indicating a possible subspecies distributed in the state of Perak (Abdul-Latiff and Md-Zain 2021).

Sousa plumbea has been recognized as a species since taxonomic revision of the genus Sousa in 2014 (Committee on Taxonomy 2014, Jefferson and Rosenbaum 2014). Previously this species was lumped together with the Indo-Pacific Humpback Dolphin (Sousa chinensis), but animals occurring in the western part of the Indian Ocean from South Africa to western coastline of India are now recognized as taxonomically distinct from those that occur further east, based on genetics, skeletal morphology, external morphology and colour. There is uncertainty about the taxonomic affinities of the Humpback Dolphins that occur in the Bay of Bengal. Animals in this area do not appear to be S. plumbea or S. chinensis, or to be hybrids of the two, and may be a separate taxonomic entity (Amaral et al. 2017; 2020). Additional sampling should confirm the eastern limits of the S. plumbea species, but for the purposes of this assessment we assume it to be Sri Lanka and the southern tip of India in the Gulf of Mannar.
This is the only representative of its genus.
The species is closely related to Rattus hoffmanni. Further taxonomic research is needed as there is some uncertainty surrounding its status.

A recently published taxonomic review (Miranda et al. 2023) proposes to split the northern (in southern Bahia and Sergipe) and southern subpopulations (Espírito Santo and Rio de Janeiro) into two species - Bradypus torquatus (Bahia and Sergipe) and Bradypus crinitus (Rio de Janeiro and Espírito Santo), respectively. The subpopulations were already considered genetically distinct, according to previous studies (Lara-Ruiz et al. 2008, Schetino et al. 2017). This recent analysis (Miranda et al. 2023), based on coalescent delimitation using mitochondrial and nuclear DNA, morphological analyses and field observations, recognizes that they are in fact two species, B. torquatus and B. crinitus.The Maned Sloth was occasionally classified as a subgenus of Bradypus or placed in his own genus, Scaeopus (Hayssen 2009, Wetzel and Ávila-Pires 1980) due to its morphological distinctiveness. A growing number of molecular studies supports this differentiation (Gibb et al. 2016, Schetino et al. 2017, Ruiz-Garcia et al. 2020), but additional studies are needed to confirm this.

A recently published taxonomic review (Miranda et al. 2023) proposes to split the northern (in southern Bahia and Sergipe) and southern subpopulations (Espírito Santo and Rio de Janeiro) into two species - Bradypus torquatus (Bahia and Sergipe) and Bradypus crinitus (Rio de Janeiro and Espírito Santo), respectively. The subpopulations were already considered genetically distinct, according to previous studies (Lara-Ruiz et al. 2008, Schetino et al. 2017). This recent analysis (Miranda et al. 2023), based on coalescent delimitation using mitochondrial and nuclear DNA, morphological analyses and field observations, recognizes that they are in fact two species, B. torquatus and B. crinitus.The Maned Sloth was occasionally classified as a subgenus of Bradypus or placed in his own genus, Scaeopus (Hayssen 2009, Wetzel and Ávila-Pires 1980) due to its morphological distinctiveness. A growing number of molecular studies supports this differentiation (Gibb et al. 2016, Schetino et al. 2017, Ruiz-Garcia et al. 2020), but additional studies are needed to confirm this.

The name Bos taurus was used by Carl Linnaeus in 1758 for feral cattle. The scientific name Bos primigenius was proposed for the Aurochs by Ludwig Bojanus in 1825 (later dated to 1827). The International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature (2003) placed Bos primigenius on the Official List of Specific Names in Zoology and thereby recognized the validity of this name for the wild species.Three subspecies are recognised B. p. primigenius (Eurasia), B. p. mauritanicus (North Africa), B. p. namadicus (South Asian subcontinent) (Van Vuure 2005). Archaeozoological and genetic data indicate that taurine cattle Bos taurus were first domesticated from local Aurochs (B. p. primigenius) in the Near East around 10,500 years ago and that a second domestication (of B. p. namadicus) took place about 1500 years later in India, giving rise to the zebu Bos indicus line (Bollongino et al. 2012; Park et al. 2015; Pitt et al. 2019).

Myosorex eisentrauti was previously considered to include M. okuensis and M. rumpii (see Hutterer and Peters 2010), though both are now recognised as distinct species (Mammal Diversity Database 2023).

The Indus River Dolphin (Platanista minor Owen, 1853), is one of two recognized species in the family Platanistidae (Braulik et al. 2015a,  2021). From the late 1990s until 2021, the Indus River Dolphin and its sister species, the Ganges River Dolphin (Platanista gangetica (Lebeck, 1801)), were recognized and listed on the Red List as subspecies P. g. minor and P. g. gangetica, respectively (Rice 1998, Society for Marine Mammalogy 2021). In early 2021, evidence from a comparison of mtDNA control region sequences together with external and skeletal morphology was published, indicating substantial differences between Indus and Ganges dolphins that warranted a change in taxonomy to separate species (Braulik et al. 2015a, 2021). This change was approved by the Committee on Taxonomy of the Society for Marine Mammalogy, the generally recognized authority, in mid-2021 (Committee of Taxonomy 2021). Details on the taxonomic history of the two species are summarized in Braulik et al. (2021).

Two subspecies have been described (Duplaix 1980): (1) Pteronura brasiliensis brasiliensis (Gmelin, 1788) from Suriname, the Guianas, southern Venezuela, southern Colombia, eastern Ecuador, eastern Peru, Bolivia, Paraguay, and Brazil; and (2) P. b. paranensis (Rengger, 1830) from the Paraguay and Parana rivers in Brazil, northern Argentina and Uruguay. However, this subspecific division was rejected by Carter and Rosas (1997) and described as being of “doubtful value” by Duplaix (1980).  Wozencraft (2005) treated subspecies paranensis as a synonym of a different subspecies: P. b. paraguensis (Schinz, 1821). Subsequent mitochondrial DNA and microsatellite analysis supports subdivision of the species into four evolutionary distinct units (ESUs). These comprise: (1) the Rio Madre de Dios with the Rio Madeira (2); the Pantanal (3) the Amazon with the Orinoco and Guianas drainages (4) The Itenez/Guapore basin (Garcia et al. 2007; Pickles et al. 2011, 2012).
This recently described species (Puechmaille et al. 2023) was formerly one of the species (Myotis sp. C., subpopulation endemic to Corsica) belonging to the Myotis nattereri complex.

Pygmy Lorises were deemed a distinct genus (Xanthonycticebus) from other Slow Loris taxa (Nycticebus) on the basis of have diverged approximately 11 million years ago, as well as traits including regularly giving birth to twins, having black naked ears, and a smaller skull length than Nycticebus (Nekaris and Nijman, 2022; Blair et al., 2023). Pygmy lorises are relatively small (360-580 g); black nose; the fur on the crown patch of the head is orangish to light brown and is diffuse to ears with eye patches rather than sharply forked. Southern Pygmy Lorises are distinct from Northern Pygmy Lorises on the basis of a skull (55 mm or less) and mandible (35 mm or less) length larger than intermedius, resulting in a longer “dog-like” muzzle; pelage is comprised of shorter hairs, and there is less distinct coat colour change through the seasons (Blair et al., 2023).  There is no evidence of hybridization, either in the wild or in museum specimens, between N. bengalensis and X. pygmaeus from localities where the two are sympatric.

Pygmy Lorises were deemed a distinct genus (Xanthonycticebus) from other Slow Loris taxa (Nycticebus) based on have diverged approximately 11 million years ago, as well as other distinct traits including regularly giving birth to twins, having black naked ears, and a smaller skull length than Nycticebus (Nekaris and Nijman, 2022; Blair et al., 2023). Pygmy Lorises are relatively small (360-580 g); black nose; the fur on the crown patch of the head is orangish to light brown and is diffuse to ears with eye patches rather than sharply forked. Northern Pygmy Lorises are distinct from Southern Pygmy Lorises on the basis of a smaller skull length (50 mm or less), smaller mandible length (33 mm or less) resulting in a shorter muzzle; relatively fluffier pelage with longer hair (Blair et al., 2023). They also go through a more distinct seasonal coat colour and body mass change (Streicher, 2004).  There is no evidence of hybridization, either in the wild or in museum specimens, between N. bengalensis and X. pygmaeus from localities where the two are sympatric.

Cooper et al. (2003) reported that there is only limited genetic difference between extant subpopulations from southeastern and southwestern Australia. However a subsequent study using microsatellite and mitochondrial DNA identified differences and proposed that the species comprises two highly-divergent lineages either side of the Nullarbor Plain that should be defined as separate Evolutionary Significant Units (ESUs) (Salinas et al. 2009). No subspecies are recognised.
No subspecies are recognised.
No subspecies are recognised. Mahoney (1977) found that the single skull was distinct from other Notomys and that it was likely to be most closely related to Mitchell’s Hopping Mouse N. mitchellii.

This taxon was elevated to full species status by Hutterer (2005). Originally it was regarded as "a local variety" of the southeast Asian species Crocidura fuliginosa. Based on morphological characteristics, Jenkins (1976) described it as a subspecies of C. attenuata. Corbet and Hill (1992) questioned the validity of it being conspecific with C. attenuata based on morphological characteristics. Ruedi (1995) studied 11 specimens and concluded that they were different from C. attenuata. With further analysis, Eldridge et al. (2014) corroborated its specific status.
No subspecies are recognized.

authority: Timm, Weijola, K.P. Aplin, Flannery & Pine, 2016

taxonomicNotes: No subspecies are recognised

No subspecies are recognised. Perameles eremiana was closely related to the P. bougainville complex and it has been proposed that they are clinal forms of the same species (Friedman 1967); however, Westerman et al. (2012) recommended maintaining them as separate species and Travouillon and Phillips (2018), in a major study of bandicoot relationships, also maintained it as a separate species.

Groves in Wilson and Reeder (1993) considered this to be a synonym of B. penicillata, however Nowak (1999) and Jackson and Groves (2015) recognised its specific status, which is also followed by the Australasian Mammal Taxonomy Consortium.

Originally described as a subspecies of B. penicillata (Finlayson 1957), the Desert Bettong has only recently been recognized as a full species (McDowell et al. 2015). Finlayson (1958) provided a more detailed description of the skull and speculated that it represented a full species. However, Wakefield (1967) later synonymised anhydra with B. lesueur, based on his opinion that several features of the skull were similar to B. lesueur, although he noted that ‘the skull is very small’ (Wakefield 1967, p. 11). The Desert Bettong can be separated from the Woylie B. penicillata and the Boodie B. lesueur by several skull and dentition characters (McDowell 2013). Ancient DNA (aDNA) analysis of the Cytochrome b gene from the holotype shows it is a well-supported lineage, distinct enough from its closest relatives B. penicillata and B. lesueur to consider it a separate species (McDowell et al. 2015).
No subspecies are recognised
No subspecies are recognised.
No subspecies are recognised.
No subspecies have been described.
No subspecies have been described.

This species was first described in 2017 (Nater et al. 2017). The Nater et al. study showed that an isolated orangutan population in the Batang Toru region, which is the southernmost range of extant Sumatran Orangutans (Pongo abelii), south of Lake Toba, is distinct from other Sumatran and Bornean populations. Through a comparison of cranio-mandibular and dental characters from an orangutan killed during human-orangutan conflict to 33 adult male orangutans of similar developmental stage, the study found consistent differences between the Batang Toru individual and other extant Ponginae. Model-based approaches based on the analyses of 37 orangutan genomes supported the morphological results by revealing that the deepest split in the evolutionary history of extant orangutans occurred ~3.38 Ma ago between the Batang Toru population and those to the north of Lake Toba, while both currently recognized species separated much later at about 674 ka. The analyses show that there was a small gene flow between P. abelii and P. tapanuliensis until 10–20 ka. The combined analyses support a new classification of orangutans into three extant species.

Although there is ongoing debate about the taxonomic status of the Bornean Orangutan (see review in Goossens et al. 2009); three subspecies are currently recognized for the taxon:P. p. pygmaeus: Northwest Bornean Orangutan:State of Sarawak (Malaysia)Province of West Kalimantan (Indonesia)P. p. wurmbii: Southwest Bornean Orangutan:Province of West Kalimantan (Indonesia)Province of Central Kalimantan (Indonesia)P. p. morio: Northeast Bornean Orangutan:State of Sabah (Malaysia)Province of North Kalimantan (Indonesia)Province of East Kalimantan (Indonesia)

Historically, the Sumatran and Bornean Orangutans were both considered to be subspecies of Pongo pygmaeus (e.g., Rijksen and Meijaard 1999). Recent taxonomic reviews (Groves 2001, Brandon-Jones et al. 2004) support the acceptance of the Sumatran Orangutan (Pongo abelii) as distinct from its Bornean relative (Pongo pygmaeus). This classification has since been widely adopted (e.g., Singleton et al. 2004). A recent study (Nater et al. 2017) proposed that the Batang Toru population is a separate species (Pongo tapanuliensis), and therefore this assessment does not include the orangutans in that area.
This species is monotypic (Steppan and Ramirez 2015).

Wangchuk et al. (2003) described a new subspecies, Trachypithecus geei bhutanensis, from Bhutan in the north of the range, with T. g. geei occurring in the south, the two taxa being separated by the Main Frontal Thrust (MFT) of the Indian plate hitting into the Himalayas (Bhargava 1997). However, T. g. bhutanensis is not recognised here as a valid subspecies, as it has not been described according to ICZN rules.
This taxon is sometimes regarded as a subspecies of Mogera tokudae (see Hutterer 2005), but sufficient karyotypic differences exist to warrant species recognition.
Also known as Echimys thomasi and Nelomys thomasi, but see Emmons et al. (2002) for reasons to place this form in the genus Phyllomys.

Three subspecies of Banteng have come to be generally recognised (Lekagul and McNeely 1977, National Research Council 1983): 1. Bos javanicus javanicus on Java and Bali,2. B. j. lowi on Borneo, and 3. B. j. birmanicus on the Asian mainland.Grzimek (1990) argued against separating these local forms into true subspecies because a number of the small populations of wild Banteng which remain have been affected by interbreeding with domestic or feral cattle. Corbet and Hill (1992) did not recognise any valid subspecies of Banteng either. Frequent reports of interbreeding between wild Banteng and domestic livestock on Borneo (S. Hedges pers. comm. 2008) have yet to be substantiated in Sabah and Java (J. Burton pers. comm. 2016). Phylogenetic studies of B. j. lowi are confounded by the difficulties of obtaining samples. Recent genetic studies have produced contradicting results with regards to the taxonomic status of Banteng on Borneo. Mitochondrial DNA evidence suggested that the Bornean and Javan Banteng have such large genetic distances as to constitute separate species (Ishige et al. 2016, Handayani 2023). However, nuclear genomic data refutes this hypothesis and suggests that Bornean Banteng affiliates strongly with the Javan Banteng (Sun et al. 2022).Until there is sufficient information on its genetic description, current population size, and status of B. j. lowi should not be included within B. javanicus. Pending further morphometric and genetic analysis Bos j. javanicus and B. j. birmanicus are provisionally accepted here in recognition of the relatively pronounced phenotypic differences which exist between Banteng on Java and the Asian mainland.

authority: Esselstyn, Achmadi, Handika, Swanson, Giarla, & Rowe, 2021
taxonomicNotes: The phylogenetic relationship of this species suggested a sister relationship to a clade containing Crocidura ordinaria and C. solita, both known from the Central Core of Sulawesi area of endemism (Esselstyn et al. 2021).

authority: Esselstyn, Achmadi, Handika, Swanson, Giarla, & Rowe, 2021
taxonomicNotes: Described by Esselstyn et al. (2021) as a new species of the 'Small-Bodied Group', a morphogroup of shrews found on Sulawesi.
Two subspecies are recognized: Coleura seychellensis seychellensis from Mahé and Praslin, and C. s. silhouettae from Silhouette and La Digue (Hill 1971, Rocamora and Joubert 2004).

Macaca fascicularis is a wide-ranging macaque with a patchy distribution in South and Southeast Asia (Eudey 2008, Gumert, 2011). According to a variety of descriptions (Raffles 1821, Fooden 1995, 2006, Groves 2001), they are not distinguished by any distinct phenotypic features; however, they are one of the smaller macaques, and are named for their long tail, which approximates the length of their body and head combined. From a distance, the dorsal pelage is generally brown or greyish, with a whitish underbelly, as well as distinct black and white highlights around the crown and face. For example, their eyelids are often white, their crowns are bordered by white or black, and distinct white spots often occur on the skin in front of their ears. Closer inspection of the pelage reveals a mottled or speckled pattern, due to the individual hairs being of different colour. This hair patterning produces variation in shading between individuals and across populations. Hair shading includes yellowish, golden brown, reddish brown, brown, whitish, and blackened. Their skin coloration varies and includes white, pink, black, and brown tones. Under harsh sunlight, their facial skin easily tans. Adults are usually bearded on and around their face; however, around their snout and eyes, hair is short and sparse. Older females typically exhibit the fullest bearding, while for males, it is more whisker-like. They exhibit a lateral cheek crest that usually runs vertically along the face, but among some subspecific variations, the crest runs horizontally, bisecting the middle of the cheek. There are distinct differences in pelage colour between the different subspecies, ranging from almost black to light brown/grey.The species is highly variable. Groves (2001) and Fooden (1995) have divided Macaca fascicularis into ten subspecies; however, as of recent, the most eastern subspecies, M. f. philippinensis has come under dispute (Smith et al. 2014). Consequently, it has been tentatively removed from the Red List assessment and is currently included with M. f. fascicularis. Along the northern part of their range, M. fascicularis regularly hybridizes with the Rhesus Macaque, M. mulatta (Fooden 1964). They have also long been known to hybridize with the Southern Pig-tailed Macaque, M. nemestrina (Bernstein 1966). Hybrids can also occur across subspecies (Bunlungsup et al. 2016, Gumert et al. 2019).In regard to synonyms*: Simia aygula Linnaus 1758, Simia cynomolgus Schreber 1775, Simia fascicularis Raffles 1821, Macacus carbonaria Cuvier, 1825, Macaca irus Geoffroy 1826, Semnopithecus kra Lesson 1830, and numerous other names have been ascribed using the above genera, as well as genera assignments that have also included Macac, Pithecus, and Cynomolgus.*There are numerous historical synonyms and mistaken names for this species. Early uses are highlighted here, but for a more complete historical listing and discussion, please refer to Napier and Groves (1983), Groves (2001), and Fooden (1995).
Rhinolophus hilli has been recognized as a distinct species (Fahr et al. 2002, Simmons and Cirranello 2021).
Three subspecies exist in Mexico: Peromyscus guardia guardia, P. g. harbisoni, and P. g. mejiae (Mellink and Luévano 2014).

Pteropus livingstonii has been allied to P. melanopogon (Nowak 1999), but it was later suggested that P. livingstonii and P. melanopogon are separate monotypic groups (Simmons 2005). Most recent genetic studies suggest P. livingstonii is a sister species to P. voeltzkowi (O’Brien et al. 2009, Chan et al. 2011). The suggested synonym P. livinstonei is an unjustified emendation by Anderson 1912 of P. livingstonii Gray 1866 (Smith and Leslie 2006).

The only other known species in this genus is Halmaheramys bokimekot (Fabre et al. 2013). Further research is needed to determine whether morphological differences between the individuals found on Obi Island and Bisa Island could indicate taxonomic divergence (Fabre et al. 2018).

authority: Nguyen Truong Son, Oshida, Dang Huy Phuong, Bui Tuan Hai, & Motokawa, 2018
taxonomicNotes: Callosciurus honkhoaiensis is small compared to other species of the genus. It is closely related to Callosciurus caniceps (Nguyen et al. 2018).
It is the only member of the monotypic genus Chingawaemys (Nicolas et al. 2021).

This species is closely related to Habromys chinanteco and H. simulatus. However, strong morphometric differentiation and geographic isolation help to differentiate the species (Romo-Vázquez et al. 2005).

Morphometric and allozyme analyses, as well as microsatellite profiling, have suggested that Hooded Seals are a single panmictic population (Wiig and Lie 1984, Sundt et al. 1994, Coltman et al. 2007). However, body size differences, behavioural differences and the breeding site fidelity displayed by populations, make further genetics investigations warranted. Currently, management is conducted for two regions (Sergeant 1974, Lavigne and Kovacs 1988): 1) the Northwest Atlantic group, which has traditionally given birth in three areas - Davis Strait, the southern Gulf of St Lawrence and off of northeastern Newfoundland (an area known as the “Front”), Canada, and 2) Hooded Seals from the Northeast Atlantic that breed on ice east of Greenland, in the Greenland Sea, northwest of the island of Jan Mayen (Rice 1998).

Previously recognised as belonging to a monotypic genus Oreailurus, primarily based on the relative size difference in the skull's auditory chambers obtained from very few specimens (Cabrera 1940, Wozencraft 1993, Nowell and Jackson 1996, Yensen and Seymour 2000). Subsequently, based on genetic evidence, the Andean Cat was included in the genus Leopardus. The specific name, jacobita, is in honour of Jacobita Mantagazza (Cornalia 1865) and should not be declined to jacobitus as is sometimes seen in the literature (Sanderson 2015).
